kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  4 be an integer (no parity restriction) and let a be any non-zero real number.  
For x \in  \mathbb{R} put  

  x^{\overline{m}}:=x(x+1)(x+2)\cdots(x+m-1)  (m \in  \mathbb{N})  

(the m-th rising factorial, so that x^{\overline{m}}=\Gamma (x+m)/\Gamma (x)).  
For every real number y define  

  W_n,_a(y)=\sum _{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k}\binom{n}{k}\Bigl[\,
                \pi \,(\,y+ak)^{\overline{\,n\,}}
              +e\,(\,y+ak+2)^{\overline{\,n-3\,}}
              +\ln 2\Bigr].                                       (\star )

(a) Show that W_n,_a(y) is independent of y.  
(b) Prove the closed form  

  W_n,_a(y)=\pi \,(-a)^{\,n}\,n!.                                                (**)

(Hint: interpret (\star ) as the n-th forward difference of a degree-n polynomial but beware of the overall sign of (1-E_a)^n.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout let  

  (\Delta _{n,a}f)(t):=(1-E_{a})^{n}f(t)
        =\sum _{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k}\binom{n}{k}f(t+ak),  E_{a}f(t):=f(t+a).  (1)

With  

  f(t):=\pi \,t^{\overline{n}}+e\,(t+2)^{\overline{\,n-3\,}}+\ln 2         (2)

we have exactly W_n,_a(y)=\Delta _{n,a}f(y).

Step 1 -  The n-th difference of a rising factorial.  
Claim. For every real t and every a\neq 0  

  \Delta _{n,a}\bigl(t^{\overline{n}}\bigr)=(-a)^{\,n}\,n!.  (3)

Proof.  
Because t^{\overline{n}} is a monic polynomial of degree n, write  

 t^{\overline{n}}=\sum _{j=0}^{n}c_{j}t^{j}  (c_{n}=1).

Applying (1-E_{a})^{n} and extracting the leading term gives  

 \Delta _{n,a}t^{\overline{n}}=\sum _{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k}\binom{n}{k}(t+ak)^{n}+(\text{terms of degree }<n).

The inner sum equals (1-E_{a})^{n}t^{n}, which in turn equals  
 (1-E_{a})^{n}t^{n}=(-a)^{n}n!                                              (4)  

(the classical formula for the n-th difference of x^{n} with operator (1-E_{a})).  
All lower-degree terms vanish because \sum _{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k}\binom{n}{k}k^{j}=0 for j<n.  
Therefore (3) holds. \blacksquare 

Remark.  Had we used the more customary operator (E_{a}-1)^{n}, the sign would be a^{n}n!.  The present definition differs by a factor (-1)^{n}, hence formula (3).

Step 2 -  Vanishing of lower-degree pieces.  

(i) g_1(t)=t^{\overline{n}}  (degree n).  
(ii) g_2(t)=(t+2)^{\overline{\,n-3\,}}  (degree n-3 < n).  
(iii) g_3(t)=1.

For any polynomial of degree m<n we have \Delta _{n,a}(\text{that polynomial})=0, because (1-E_{a})^{n} annihilates every polynomial of degree <n.  Hence  

 \Delta _{n,a}\bigl[e\,g_2\bigr](y)=0,  \Delta _{n,a}\bigl[\ln 2\bigr](y)=0.  (5)

Step 3 -  Putting the parts together.  
Using (3) and (5) in (1)-(2),

 W_n,_a(y)=\pi \cdot \Delta _{n,a}(g_1)(y)=\pi \cdot (-a)^{n}n!.  (6)

The right-hand side contains no y, which simultaneously proves

(a) W_n,_a(y) is y-independent, and  
(b) W_n,_a(y)=\pi (-a)^{\,n}n!, i.e. (**) holds.

Observation.  If n is even the sign disappears and the value reduces to \pi a^{n}n!, recovering the version stated in the original draft.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher‐degree structure.  
   • The core term (y²+y(k−1)+k²)^{n/2} is built from a quadratic in k and y, then raised to the power n/2, producing a degree-n polynomial whose leading monic coefficient must be identified.  
   • Recognising that this expression is still monic of exact degree n is essential and is subtler than in the original problem where the leading term is manifestly (±k)^{n}.

2. Several additional components.  
   • A second polynomial of degree n−4 and a pure constant are present; one has to know (and prove) that an n-th finite difference annihilates them.  
   • This forces the solver to distinguish carefully which pieces survive and which vanish after Δₙ is applied.

3. Use of finite–difference operators.  
   • The problem now explicitly requires operator algebra (E-shift, Δ, powers of Δ) to justify the conclusions rigorously, not merely the “nth difference of a monic polynomial” slogan.

4. Necessity of a full degree argument.  
   • Because the first summand is disguised inside a power of a quadratic, seeing that its leading coefficient is still 1 and of exactly degree n demands deliberate expansion or a theorem on leading terms, unlike the original k-shift monomial.

5. Combined reasoning.  
   • The solver must blend polynomial degree analysis, properties of forward differences, and an operator perspective (or Stirling-number machinery) into a coherent proof.  
   • A careless attempt that ignores any one of these strands (for example, overlooking the monic leading term or miscounting the degree of P₂) produces the wrong result.

Overall, the variant keeps the fundamental idea (an n-th alternating sum of binomial-weighted values) but embeds it in a far more intricate algebraic environment, demands precise operator knowledge, and introduces several interacting terms that must be treated separately—making it **significantly harder** than both the original and the current kernel versions.

              +e\,(\,y+ak+2)^{\overline{\,n-3\,}}
              +\ln 2\Bigr].                                       (\star )

  W_n,_a(y)=\pi \,(-a)^{\,n}\,n!.                                                (**)
